title: Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil

seo.title: Authentic Focaccia Genovese Recipe with Rosemary & Olive Oil
seo.metaDescription: Learn to make authentic Focaccia Genovese with rosemary and olive oil. This easy Italian recipe brings the taste of Genoa to your kitchen. Perfect for any meal!
longTailKeywords: easy Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil
lowCompetitionKeywords: homemade Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil
seo.h1: Authentic Focaccia Genovese with Rosemary & Olive Oil
h2Headings: Ingredients You Need for Focaccia Genovese
Step-by-Step Instructions to Make Focaccia Genovese
Pro Tips for the Best Focaccia Genovese
Variations and Substitutions for Focaccia Genovese
seo.imageAlt: Freshly baked Focaccia Genovese with fragrant rosemary and glistening olive oil, golden brown and airy.
seo.imageCaption: Indulge in the simple perfection of Focaccia Genovese, a classic Italian bread infused with rosemary and olive oil.

description: Focaccia Genovese is a classic Italian flatbread, celebrated for its airy texture, crisp crust, and rich olive oil flavor. Infused with aromatic rosemary and a touch of sea salt, this traditional recipe transports you straight to the Ligurian coast. Perfect as an appetizer, side dish, or light meal, it embodies the simple elegance of Italian baking.

instructions.title: Activate the Yeast
instructions.text: In a large mixing bowl, combine the warm water, sugar, and active dry yeast. Stir gently and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until the mixture becomes foamy, indicating the yeast is active.

instructions.title: Prepare the Dough
instructions.text: Add the all-purpose flour and fine sea salt to the yeast mixture. Mix with a sturdy wooden spoon or use a stand mixer with a dough hook attachment until a shaggy, cohesive dough forms.

instructions.title: Knead the Dough
instructions.text: Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface. Knead by hand for 8-10 minutes until the dough is smooth, elastic, and no longer sticky. If using a stand mixer, knead on medium speed for 5-7 minutes.

instructions.title: First Rise
instructions.text: Lightly coat a clean bowl with a drizzle of extra virgin olive oil. Place the kneaded dough in the bowl, turning it once to coat all sides with oil. Cover the bowl tightly with plastic wrap and let it rise in a warm place for 1 to 1.5 hours, or until it has doubled in size.

instructions.title: Prepare for Second Rise
instructions.text: Gently deflate the risen dough and transfer it to a 9x13 inch baking pan that has been generously coated with extra virgin olive oil. Gently stretch the dough to fill the pan. Cover the pan again with plastic wrap and let it rise for another 30-45 minutes in a warm spot. Preheat your oven to 220C (425F) during this time.

instructions.title: Dimple and Top
instructions.text: Once the dough has completed its second rise, remove the plastic wrap. Use your fingertips to create deep indentations all over the surface of the dough. Drizzle generously with more extra virgin olive oil, then sprinkle evenly with the chopped fresh rosemary and coarse sea salt.

instructions.title: Bake the Focaccia
instructions.text: Place the baking pan in the preheated oven. Bake for 20-30 minutes, or until the focaccia is golden brown on top and cooked through. If the top browns too quickly, you can loosely tent it with aluminum foil.

instructions.title: Cool and Serve
instructions.text: Carefully remove the focaccia from the oven. Transfer it to a wire rack to cool slightly before slicing. Serve warm as an appetizer, alongside a meal, or simply enjoy it on its own.

tips: For the most authentic texture, ensure your dough is well-hydrated and kneaded until very elastic. The long fermentation helps develop complex flavors.
Use high-quality extra virgin olive oil, especially for the final drizzle, as its flavor is central to Focaccia Genovese. Fresh rosemary is highly recommended over dried.
Focaccia is best enjoyed warm on the day it is baked. If you have leftovers, lightly toast them to revive the crispness and aroma.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the sun-drenched Ligurian coast of Italy, where one of the most beloved and simple breads originated: Focaccia Genovese. This isn't just any focaccia; it's a masterpiece of texture and flavor, celebrated for its impossibly airy crumb, delicate crisp crust, and the intoxicating aroma of rosemary and high-quality olive oil. More than just a bread, it’s an experience, a staple that graces tables from humble trattorias to elegant dinner parties.

The beauty of Focaccia Genovese lies in its straightforward ingredients and a technique that, while requiring a little patience, yields incredibly rewarding results. It’s a testament to how simple elements, expertly combined, can create something truly extraordinary. If you’ve ever dreamed of baking a piece of Italy in your own kitchen, perfecting the Focaccia Genovese recipe is an excellent place to start. Get ready to fill your home with the irresistible scent of fresh Italian baking.

## What is Focaccia Genovese?

Focaccia Genovese, or Fûgassa in the Ligurian dialect, is a flatbread originating from Genoa, the capital city of Italy’s Liguria region. Unlike its thicker, often more bread-like counterparts found in other parts of Italy, Genovese focaccia is distinctly known for its thin, crisp bottom, incredibly soft and airy interior, and a top generously dimpled to hold pools of glistening olive oil and coarse sea salt.

The defining characteristics of this particular style include a high-hydration dough, which contributes to its light, open crumb, and an unsparing use of extra virgin olive oil – both incorporated into the dough and drizzled lavishly over the top before baking. Traditional Focaccia Genovese is often enjoyed plain, or simply adorned with coarse sea salt and fresh rosemary, allowing the quality of the olive oil and the bread’s unique texture to shine. It’s a versatile bread, perfect for dipping in olive oil, serving alongside cured meats and cheeses, or simply eaten on its own as a snack. Its historical significance as a staple food in Genoa dates back centuries, making it not just food, but a piece of cultural heritage.

## Ingredients You Need for Focaccia Genovese

Crafting the perfect Focaccia Genovese recipe begins with selecting high-quality ingredients. Each plays a crucial role in achieving that authentic Ligurian flavor and texture.

*   **Strong Bread Flour:** Approximately 500 grams. A high-protein bread flour is essential for developing strong gluten, which gives the focaccia its characteristic chewiness and open, airy crumb.
*   **Active Dry Yeast:** 7 grams (about 2 ¼ teaspoons). This is your leavening agent, crucial for the dough's rise and airy texture.
*   **Warm Water:** Around 400-450 ml (about 1 ¾ to 2 cups). The exact amount can vary slightly depending on your flour and environment, but a high hydration is key. The water should be lukewarm (around 105-115°F or 40-46°C) to activate the yeast.
*   **Extra Virgin Olive Oil:** 60-80 ml for the dough (about ¼ to ⅓ cup), plus extra for the pan and generous drizzling on top. This is perhaps the most critical ingredient. Invest in a good quality, fruity extra virgin olive oil; it truly makes all the difference in flavor.
*   **Fine Sea Salt:** 10 grams (about 1 ½ teaspoons) for the dough.
*   **Coarse Sea Salt:** For sprinkling on top, adding texture and bursts of briny flavor. Flaky sea salt also works wonderfully.
*   **Fresh Rosemary:** A few sprigs, leaves stripped and roughly chopped, for an aromatic topping.

With these simple, yet potent, ingredients, you are well on your way to mastering the Focaccia Genovese recipe.

## Step-by-Step Instructions to Make Focaccia Genovese

Follow these steps carefully to create an authentic and delicious Focaccia Genovese. This Focaccia Genovese recipe prioritizes technique for optimal results.

1.  **Activate the Yeast:** In a large bowl, combine the warm water and yeast. Stir gently and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until foamy, indicating the yeast is active.
2.  **Mix the Dough:** Add the bread flour and fine sea salt to the yeast mixture. Pour in 60ml of the extra virgin olive oil. Mix with a wooden spoon or your hands until a shaggy dough forms and no dry streaks of flour remain. The dough will be very sticky, which is normal for a high-hydration focaccia.
3.  **Knead (or Fold) the Dough:** For this high-hydration dough, a gentle stretch-and-fold method is often preferred over traditional kneading.
    *   Lightly oil your work surface and your hands.
    *   Scrape the dough onto the surface. Gently stretch one side of the dough away from you, then fold it back over itself. Rotate the dough 90 degrees and repeat. Do this for about 5-7 minutes.
    *   Alternatively, if using a stand mixer with a dough hook, knead on medium-low speed for about 8-10 minutes until the dough is smooth and elastic.
4.  **First Rise (Bulk Fermentation):** Transfer the dough to a generously oiled bowl, turning it to coat all sides. Cover the bowl tightly with plastic wrap or a damp kitchen towel. Let it rise in a warm place for 1.5 to 2 hours, or until doubled in size. For a deeper flavor, you can also cold-proof it in the refrigerator overnight (8-12 hours).
5.  **Prepare the Pan:** Liberally drizzle 2-3 tablespoons of olive oil into a 9x13 inch (23x33 cm) baking pan. Ensure the bottom and sides are well coated.
6.  **Transfer and Shape:** Gently deflate the risen dough and carefully transfer it to the prepared baking pan. With oiled hands, gently stretch the dough to fill the pan, being careful not to tear it. It might resist at first; if so, let it rest for 10 minutes, then continue stretching.
7.  **Second Rise (Proofing in Pan):** Once the dough roughly fills the pan, cover it again with plastic wrap or a damp towel. Let it rise for another 30-45 minutes in a warm spot, or until noticeably puffy.
8.  **Dimple and Top:** Preheat your oven to 425°F (220°C). Once the dough has proofed, remove the cover. Generously drizzle another 2-3 tablespoons of olive oil over the surface of the dough. Using your oiled fingertips, gently but firmly press deep indentations all over the surface of the focaccia. These dimples are crucial for holding the olive oil and creating the characteristic texture. Sprinkle generously with coarse sea salt and fresh rosemary.
9.  **Bake:** Bake for 20-25 minutes, or until the focaccia is golden brown on top and the bottom is crisp and golden.
10. **Cool:** Carefully remove the focaccia from the pan and transfer it to a wire rack to cool slightly. Serve warm.

This detailed Focaccia Genovese recipe ensures a truly authentic experience.

## Pro Tips for the Best Focaccia Genovese

Achieving that perfect, authentic Focaccia Genovese requires attention to a few key details. These pro tips will elevate your Focaccia Genovese recipe from good to extraordinary.

*   **Quality Olive Oil is Non-Negotiable:** We cannot stress this enough. The flavor of your focaccia is profoundly influenced by the quality of your extra virgin olive oil. Use a good, fruity, robust olive oil for both the dough and the topping.
*   **Embrace the Stickiness:** A high-hydration dough is what gives Focaccia Genovese its incredibly airy, open crumb. Don't be tempted to add more flour if the dough feels sticky. Oiled hands are your best friend for handling it.
*   **Patience with Proofing:** Allow ample time for both the first and second rises. Slow fermentation develops deeper flavors and a better texture. Don't rush it, especially if you opt for a cold proof overnight – it's worth the wait.
*   **Proper Dimpling:** Don't be shy when dimpling the dough. Use wet or oiled fingers to prevent sticking and press deeply, almost to the bottom of the pan. These wells are where the olive oil pools, creating pockets of flavor and a unique texture.
*   **Hot Oven, Hotter Results:** Bake your focaccia in a preheated, hot oven. This helps create that desirable crispy crust while keeping the interior soft and moist.
*   **Don't Overbake:** Keep an eye on the focaccia. It should be golden brown, but not dark or dry. Overbaking can lead to a tough, dry texture.
*   **Cool on a Rack:** Once out of the oven, immediately transfer the focaccia to a wire rack. This prevents the bottom from becoming soggy from trapped steam.

Following these tips will ensure your Focaccia Genovese recipe yields a consistently delicious result every time.

## Serving Suggestions for Focaccia Genovese

The beauty of Focaccia Genovese lies in its versatility. While utterly delicious on its own, especially when warm and fresh from the oven, it also serves as a fantastic accompaniment to a wide array of dishes.

*   **Simply Dipped:** The most classic way to enjoy it is torn into pieces and dipped into a small bowl of high-quality extra virgin olive oil.
*   **With Antipasti:** Pair it with a selection of Italian cured meats like prosciutto or salami, various cheeses (Parmesan, Pecorino, fresh mozzarella), marinated olives, and roasted vegetables.
*   **Alongside Soups and Salads:** Its light, airy texture and savory flavor make it an excellent companion to hearty minestrone, tomato soup, or a fresh green salad.
*   **Sandwich Bread:** Slice a piece of focaccia horizontally and fill it with your favorite sandwich ingredients. Its robust structure holds up well to various fillings.
*   **Breakfast or Snack:** In Italy, it's not uncommon to enjoy focaccia for breakfast, perhaps with a strong coffee or cappuccino. It also makes for a wonderful mid-afternoon snack.

No matter how you choose to enjoy it, this Focaccia Genovese recipe is sure to impress.

## Variations and Substitutions for Focaccia Genovese

While the classic Focaccia Genovese recipe with rosemary and sea salt is a masterpiece in itself, there's always room for a little creativity in the kitchen.

*   **Topping Variations:**
    *   **Olives:** Black or green olives, pitted and roughly chopped, pressed into the dimples.
    *   **Cherry Tomatoes:** Halved cherry tomatoes, pressed cut-side up into the dough.
    *   **Onions:** Thinly sliced red or white onions, lightly caramelized before adding, or simply scattered raw for a sharper flavor.
    *   **Garlic:** Thinly sliced garlic cloves, either baked directly or infused into the olive oil drizzled on top.
    *   **Other Herbs:** Thyme or oregano can be used in combination with or instead of rosemary.
    *   **Cheese:** A sprinkle of grated Parmesan or Pecorino Romano can be added in the last few minutes of baking, though this deviates from the traditional Genovese style.
*   **Flour Substitutions:** While strong bread flour is recommended for the best texture, you can experiment with a small percentage (up to 20%) of whole wheat flour for added nuttiness and fiber. However, for the true Focaccia Genovese recipe experience, stick mostly to white bread flour.
*   **Gluten-Free Focaccia:** While challenging to replicate the exact texture, gluten-free flour blends specifically designed for bread making can be used. Be aware that the crumb structure will be different, likely denser, but you can still achieve a flavorful result.
*   **Vegan Focaccia:** The traditional Focaccia Genovese recipe is naturally vegan, relying on olive oil rather than butter or other animal products. So, no substitutions are needed here!

Experimenting with these variations can add new dimensions to your baking, allowing you to personalize this classic Italian bread to your taste while still appreciating the core techniques of the Focaccia Genovese recipe.

Making Focaccia Genovese is more than just baking; it's an act of love and a connection to centuries of Italian culinary tradition. The simple ingredients, the careful process, and the irresistible aroma that fills your kitchen culminate in a bread that is both comforting and profoundly delicious. Whether you serve it as an appetizer, a side, or simply enjoy it on its own, this Focaccia Genovese recipe promises a taste of Liguria that you'll want to recreate again and again. So roll up your sleeves, embrace the stickiness, and get ready to enjoy one of Italy's finest culinary gifts. Your next culinary adventure starts with this Focaccia Genovese recipe!
